examples.text: أَصْبَحَ ٱلْإِنْتِرْنِتُ مَصْدَرًا رَئِيسِيًّا لِلْمَعْلُومَاتِ فِي عَصْرِنَا.
examples.english: The internet has become a primary source of information in our era.

examples.text:
الْمَصْدَرُ ٱسْمُ مَا سِوَى ٱلزَّمَانِ مِنْ
مَدْلُولَيِ ٱلْفِعْلِ كَأَمْنٍ مِنْ أَمِنْ
al-maṣdaru smu mā siwā z-zamāni min
examples.english: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

etymology.text: Noun of place from صَدَرَ (ṣadara, “to happen, to occur, to go out”).

body.text: [from water, and from a country or place, and (assumed tropical:)  from an affair or thing]. (TA. [See 1, first sentence.]) ―         -b2-  [Hence,
He knows the ways of betaking himself to things
affairs, and the ways of withdrawing himself from them;
of commencing them and of completing them
from which proceed the derivatives of verbs:

(Lth, TA:) [in this sense it is a conventional term of grammar and lexicology, not belonging to the classical language; but on account of the importance of understanding its true application in lexicology, it is necessary to give here a full explanation of it: it is, agreeably with its etymology, the
accord. to the grammarians of ElBasrah; and is what I term
a noun signifying, by its original application, an accident as subsisting in,
[“ the being possessed by a jinnee ”]),
conformable to its verb, so as to comprise all the letters in that verb, either literally
[“ the act of fighting ”], which wants the
in the verb, yet wants it as to the letter only, and not virtually, wherefore it is sometimes pronounced as if with the said letter, as in
on account of the kesr of the letter before it), or
substituting another letter for any of those letters that it wants
[“ the act of promising ”], which wants the
that is in its verb as to the letter and virtually, but has
substituted for it [by way of compensation]): (from a comparison of definitions &c. in the Expos. of the “ Kitab Hodood en-Nahw ” by the author of the work thus entitled, arts.

; the Expos. of the “ Shudhoor edh-Dhahab ” by the author of the work thus entitled, section on the nouns that govern as verbs; I' Ak; &c.:) but the grammarians of El-Koofeh hold that the verb is the root, and that the
is derived from it: (I' Ak p. 148:) some
, moreover, are derived from real (as opposed to ideal) substantives, as

has the same government as its own verb: it is often, and may be at pleasure, used as an ideal subst. or abstract noun: and it is often employed in the place of an act. or a pass. part. n.: (Kull, &c.:) [when thus used as an epithet, it is employed alike as sing. and pl. and masc. and fem.:] accord. to Zj, every
used as an epithet is for
, and therefore it has no dual nor pl. [nor fem.] form. (TA voce
.) [It has also other uses, which are expl. in the grammars. Used as a
, it is sometimes made fem.; as it is also when used in the sense of a noun that is properly fem.: see
, is a term applied to [(assumed tropical:)
a noun which is not a
but which is occasionally used in the place of a
is used in the place of an act. part. n., and in that of a pass. part. n.: such as
[“ the performing of the ablution preparatory to prayer ”], and

[“ the washing of oneself ”]; each of which wants somewhat that is in its verb without substituting anything for that which is wanting. (Expos. of the “ Kitáb el- Hodood, ” cited above.) This kind of noun the grammarians of El-Koofeh and Baghdád allow to govern as a

; but the grammarians of ElBasrah hold that the noun governed in the accus. case in each of the exs. adduced by the former as confirmatory of their opinion is so governed by a verb understood. (Expos. of the “ Shudhoor, ” ubi suprà.) It is also applied to
A proper name signifying an accident
, proper names, by original application, for
[“ vice ” and “ praise ”] and the like: and this kind does not govern as a
. (Expos. of the “ Kitáb Hodood enNahw, ” ubi suprà; and Expos. of the “ Shudhoor, ” ubi suprà.) It is also applied to [what is more properly termed
a noun applied to signify an accident
signifying “ return; ” and this kind is commonly termed in the lexicons simply an
]. (Kull p. 327.) Some apply it also to what is [properly] termed
. (Expos. of the “ Shudhoor, ” ubi suprà.) And some of the grammarians [and of the lexicographers likewise] apply it to
A noun that signifies the instrument
which the action signified by a
[“ food, ” as being “ that by means of which the act of eating (
) is performed ”]. (Kull, ubi suprà.) ―         -b5-  See also

entries.body_html: المصدر: هو الاسم الذي اشتق منه الفعل وصدر عنه.
